name: Minchiate — The 97-Card Florentine Tarot
description:
# Minchiate

## At a glance

A Florentine super-sized tarot game: **97 cards** with **40 trumps** instead of 22 —
adding the zodiac, the four elements, and the remaining theological/cardinal virtues.
Despite all that cosmic imagery it is a **game**, hugely popular for three centuries,
and a flagship of the **A-order** branch.

## Origin & dating

First recorded as **germini** in **1506** (named for the Gemini card, highest of the new
trumps). The expanded 97-card pack so outsold the older form that the older name
*minchiate* migrated to the big deck in the 1600s. From Florence it spread across Italy
and into France through the 17th century.

## Provenance & evidence

Many printed editions survive (e.g. the well-known 18th–19th-century Florentine
*Minchiate Fiorentine*). Rules are preserved in gaming literature; pagat.com documents
the game's structure. The expansion is the headline evidence: trumps nearly doubled to
absorb astrological and elemental figures.

## Game or divination?

**Game.** This is the deck that most tempts an esoteric misreading — zodiac! elements!
virtues! — yet every record treats those cards as **trump ranks in a trick-taking
game**, not oracle cards. The cosmology is decorative/encyclopedic, the same Renaissance
visual vocabulary as the Mantegna prints, not a divinatory key.

## The fear question

**No fear.** A mainstream parlor and gambling game for centuries. The familiar caveat
applies — moralists disliked *gambling* — but Minchiate as such was respectable
entertainment, openly printed and sold.

## Counter-voices

Because Minchiate looks like a "complete" astrological tarot, occult writers sometimes
present it as a survival of the *true*, larger sacred system that the 22-trump decks
supposedly censored. The evidence runs the other way: the 22-trump form is older and the
40-trump Minchiate is an **expansion for richer play**, not a recovered esoteric
original.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Minchiate's zodiac/element trumps have **no standard equivalent** in 22-trump tarot —
  the grammar should resist forcing a one-to-one mapping (the repo already flags this).
- Relationship between *minchiate* (77-card) and *germini* (97-card) naming needs a clean
  timeline in the grammar.

## Sources & further reading

- "Minchiate," Wikipedia; "Minchiate," pagat.com (rules).
- "Minchiate Fiorentine," World of Playing Cards.

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13) — the Papi.** Scholarship identifies Minchiate's five lowest trumps as I=Bagatto (Juggler), II=Grand Duke, III=Western Emperor, IV=Eastern Emperor, V=Love — there is NO Pope card and the Popess is dropped (not folded in). Where this grammar names them Popess/Empress/Emperor/Pope/Love, prefer the scholarly identities (confidence medium; verify). The suit→element pairings are a modern overlay, not historical. See [research/decks/minchiate-florence-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/decks/minchiate-florence-tarot.md).

sections.Iconography: A ragged madman wanders, sometimes with feathers and a hobby-horse. Unnumbered, he is the wild card outside the count.
sections.Meaning: Folly and freedom, the spirit outside the order; in play the Fool excuses one from following suit.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: Il Matto (The Fool) of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows the wandering jester figure — a ragged or motley-dressed figure in comic or disheveled attire, carrying a staff or bundle, with a dog or animal nipping at him. The Matto is the unnumbered outsider of the trump sequence, equivalent to the Marseille Mat and the standard tarot Fool. In the Minchiate tradition, the Matto stands outside the numbered sequence of 40 trumps, a wild card that is neither highest nor lowest but rather apart from the hierarchy entirely.
sections.Symbol: The Fool outside the numbered sequence — the jester who stands apart from the 40-trump hierarchy, folly as the position of exemption from the rules of the ordered world.

items.name: 1 — Papa One (Il Bagatto · the Juggler / “Trivial Performer”)

sections.Iconography: A seated, crowned papal figure. The first of five near-identical 'Papi'.
sections.Meaning: Minchiate dissolves the Popess, Empress, Emperor, Pope, and Love of ordinary tarot into five ranked 'Papi' (Popes) — the powers of Church and Empire flattened into one interchangeable office.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Papa Uno (Pope One) is the first of the five Papi — the Minchiate's most distinctive innovation, replacing the Papess, Pope, Emperor, Empress, and Lovers of the standard tarot with a sequence of five pope or emperor figures. In the Minchiate, the Papi are the lowest-ranking trumps (I-V), the figures of ecclesiastical and imperial authority placed at the base of the trump hierarchy rather than in the middle. Papa Uno is the first and weakest pope, his vestments less elaborate than those of the higher Papi, in the 19th-century Florentine engraving style of this 1860-1890 edition.
sections.Symbol: The First Pope — the lowest of the five Papi who replace the standard tarot's early allegorical figures, ecclesiastical authority at the base of the Minchiate trump hierarchy.

sections.Iconography: A second crowned Papa, traditionally identified with the Empress.
sections.Meaning: The second of the five Papi, carrying the old Empress. The five rank only against each other in the game.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Papa Due (Pope Two) is the second of the five Papi in the Minchiate Fiorentina — another papal or imperial figure in vestments and regalia, slightly more elaborate or more prominent than Papa Uno in the trump hierarchy. The five Papi form a unique sequence at the base of the Minchiate's 40 trumps, a quintet of authority figures not found in any other tarot tradition.
sections.Symbol: The Second Pope — the second of the Minchiate's unique quintet of authority figures, rank and regalia increasing step by step through the five Papi.

sections.Iconography: A third Papa, identified with the Emperor.
sections.Meaning: The Emperor folded into the Papi sequence — temporal power made one of five equals.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Papa Tre (Pope Three) is the third of the five Papi in the Minchiate Fiorentina — the middle figure of the papal quintet, ranked above the first two Papi in the trump hierarchy. The third Pope bears the vestments and insignia of papal or imperial authority in the 19th-century Florentine engraving tradition.
sections.Symbol: The Third Pope — the middle rank of the Minchiate's five Papi, authority increasing through the sequence of ecclesiastical figures.

sections.Iconography: A fourth Papa, the Pope proper.
sections.Meaning: Spiritual authority, the Pope, ranked fourth among the five Papi.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Papa Quattro (Pope Four) is the fourth of the five Papi in the Minchiate Fiorentina — a senior papal or imperial figure whose regalia and authority exceed the first three Papi in the trump ranking. He approaches the apex of the quintet, his vestments and insignia reflecting the increasing solemnity of the higher-ranked Papi.
sections.Symbol: The Fourth Pope — the senior figure of the Minchiate's papal quintet, approaching the apex of the five Papi with increased regalia and authority.

sections.Iconography: A fifth Papa, here carrying the old card of Love — sometimes a couple beneath Cupid.
sections.Meaning: Love absorbed into the Papi group; the highest of the five.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Papa Cinque (Pope Five) is the fifth and highest of the five Papi in the Minchiate Fiorentina — the apex of the papal quintet, the most elaborately vested and highest-ranking of the ecclesiastical authority figures that form the Minchiate's distinctive lowest trump sequence. After the five Papi, the trump sequence continues with the Cardinal and Theological Virtues.
sections.Symbol: The Fifth Pope — the apex of the Minchiate's quintet, the highest-ranked of the five authority figures before the virtues begin at trump VI.

sections.Iconography: A woman pours liquid between two vessels.
sections.Meaning: Temperance — the first of the cardinal virtues. Minchiate is the deck that carries the COMPLETE set of seven virtues.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Temperanza (Temperance) is the Cardinal Virtue of moderation at trump VI of the Minchiate Fiorentina — depicted as a female figure pouring liquid between two vessels, the conventional allegorical personification of this virtue. The Minchiate places Temperance at VI (immediately above the five Papi), and includes all four Cardinal Virtues in the trump sequence, unlike standard tarot which typically has only three or four.
sections.Symbol: Temperance at trump VI — the virtue of measured pouring, moderation as the first of the virtues above the five Papi in the Minchiate hierarchy.

sections.Iconography: A woman subdues a lion or breaks a column.
sections.Meaning: Fortitude — the cardinal virtue of courage and steadfast strength.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Forza (Fortitude / Strength) at trump VII of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the Cardinal Virtue of fortitude — depicted as a woman mastering a lion or breaking a column, the conventional allegorical personification. The Minchiate's Forza at VII follows Temperance in the virtues sequence at the base of the higher trump group.
sections.Symbol: Fortitude at trump VII — the virtue of patient moral strength, the woman mastering the lion as the second virtue above the Papi.

sections.Iconography: An enthroned woman with sword and scales.
sections.Meaning: Justice — the cardinal virtue of measure and right judgment.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Giustizia (Justice) at trump VIII of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the Cardinal Virtue of justice — depicted with sword and scales, the conventional allegorical personification of the balanced measuring of deeds. Justice at VIII in the Minchiate continues the virtues sequence, the sword and scales rendered in the 19th-century Florentine engraving style.
sections.Symbol: Justice at trump VIII — the virtue of equity and measure with sword and scales, the third virtue above the Papi in the Minchiate sequence.

sections.Iconography: A wheel turned by Fortune, figures rising and falling.
sections.Meaning: The turning of fortune, the mutability that lifts and lowers all estates.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Ruota della Fortuna (The Wheel of Fortune) at trump IX of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows the conventional turning Wheel with ascending and descending figures — the Boethian allegory of Fortune's indifferent rotation. The Wheel follows the four Cardinal Virtues in the Minchiate sequence, placed at IX as the first of the more allegorical trumps above the virtues.
sections.Symbol: The Wheel of Fortune at trump IX — Fortune's turning after the virtues, the mechanical indifference of fate following the ordering principles of the moral virtues.

sections.Iconography: A triumphal car bearing a crowned victor.
sections.Meaning: Triumph and advance — the victory parade that gives the trumps their name.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Il Carro (The Chariot) at trump X of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows a victorious figure in a triumphal vehicle — the Roman triumph allegory at the center of the trump sequence. The Carro at X is the conventional triumphal car image adapted to the Minchiate's expanded trump sequence.
sections.Symbol: The Chariot at trump X — the triumphal vehicle of victory, the Roman triumph allegory positioned at the center of the Minchiate's extended sequence.

sections.Iconography: A bent old man with a stick, sometimes an hourglass — 'Il Gobbo', the Hunchback, the Minchiate name for Time / the Hermit.
sections.Meaning: Time, age, and solitude; the bent figure measuring his hours.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Il Gobbo (The Hunchback) at trump XI of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the Minchiate's equivalent of the Hermit — a stooped or hunchbacked elderly figure with a staff, the solitary contemplative recast as a physically marked outsider. 'Gobbo' (hunchback) in Italian street culture was associated with luck, the hunchback's touch being considered fortunate in popular tradition. The Gobbo carries a lantern or staff, his stooped figure rendered in the 19th-century Florentine engraving style.
sections.Symbol: The Hunchback at trump XI — the Hermit recast as a physically marked solitary figure, wisdom and luck combined in the Italian popular tradition of the fortunate gobbo.

sections.Iconography: A youth hung by one foot from a beam.
sections.Meaning: Suspension, reversal, the traitor's shame; the world seen upside-down.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: L'Impiccato (The Hanged Man) at trump XII of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows a figure suspended by one foot from a gallows — the standard Hanged Man image of the tarot tradition, the figure's expression calm and the suspended posture suggesting willing inversion. The Impiccato at XII in the Minchiate corresponds to the Le Pendu of the Marseille tradition.
sections.Symbol: The Hanged Man at trump XII — the willing suspension and inversion of worldly values, the calm figure who has chosen the upside-down perspective.

sections.Iconography: A skeleton with a scythe among the fallen.
sections.Meaning: Death the leveller, transformation, the end that spares no estate.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Morte (Death) at trump XIII of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows the skeletal figure with scythe cutting through the living — the universal harvester in the conventional allegorical form. In the Minchiate tradition, La Morte at XIII maintains the same position as in the Marseille tarot, the anonymous skeleton who harvests without distinction of rank.
sections.Symbol: Death's skeleton with scythe at trump XIII — the universal harvester cutting through every rank indifferently, mortality at its traditional position in the Italian trump sequence.

sections.Iconography: A horned, clawed devil over bound figures.
sections.Meaning: Bondage, appetite, the force of matter that enslaves the unwary.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Il Diavolo (The Devil) at trump XIV of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows the conventional Devil figure — a winged, horned entity on a pedestal with smaller chained figures below — rendered in the 19th-century Florentine engraving tradition. The Devil at XIV in the Minchiate corresponds to the same position as in many tarot traditions.
sections.Symbol: The Devil at trump XIV — infernal authority over the chained figures below, the force of material bondage and appetite in the Minchiate's extended trump sequence.

sections.Iconography: A tower struck and burning, figures falling — 'the House of the Devil', the Minchiate Tower.
sections.Meaning: Sudden ruin, the breaking of false structures, catastrophe as release.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Casa del Diavolo (The Devil's House) at trump XV of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the Minchiate's version of the Tower card — a structure struck or destroyed, figures falling from the heights. The Minchiate calls this card the Devil's House rather than the Maison-Dieu (God's House) of the Marseille tradition, emphasizing the infernal rather than the divine source of the destruction. The falling figures and ruined structure are rendered in the 19th-century Florentine style.
sections.Symbol: The Devil's House struck at trump XV — the catastrophic destruction of false security, the Tower reframed as the house built on infernal foundations rather than struck by God.

sections.Iconography: A woman with an anchor gazes upward in prayer.
sections.Meaning: Hope — a theological virtue. With Faith and Charity it gives Minchiate the three theological virtues ordinary tarot lacks.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Speranza (Hope) at trump XVI of the Minchiate Fiorentina is one of the three Theological Virtues unique to the Minchiate and the Cary-Yale among tarot traditions. Hope is depicted as a female figure reaching upward, hands raised toward heaven or holding an anchor (the classical emblem of hope), her gaze directed above the earthly world. La Speranza at XVI continues the expanded virtues sequence of the Minchiate.
sections.Symbol: Hope at trump XVI — the theological virtue of reaching toward what is not yet present, one of three virtues unique to the Minchiate's expanded trump sequence.

sections.Iconography: A woman with a mirror and serpent, contemplating herself.
sections.Meaning: Prudence — the fourth cardinal virtue, missing from standard tarot and restored here, completing the cardinal set.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Prudenza (Prudence) at trump XVII of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the fourth Cardinal Virtue — the only one absent from most standard tarot decks (which typically include only Temperance, Fortitude, and Justice). The Minchiate's inclusion of Prudence at XVII makes it one of only two tarot traditions to include all four Cardinal Virtues. Prudence is depicted with mirror and serpent, the classical attributes of foresight and self-knowledge.
sections.Symbol: Prudence at trump XVII — the fourth Cardinal Virtue absent from most tarots, wisdom that looks before it acts rendered in the Minchiate's complete virtue sequence.

sections.Iconography: A woman with cross and chalice.
sections.Meaning: Faith — the theological virtue of trust beyond proof.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Fede (Faith) at trump XVIII of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the second Theological Virtue in the Minchiate sequence — depicted as a female figure with cross, chalice, or Apostles' Creed scroll, her gaze directed upward toward what cannot be seen. Faith at XVIII in the Minchiate's expanded trump sequence represents the belief that exceeds evidence.
sections.Symbol: Faith at trump XVIII — the theological virtue of believing without seeing, one of three Theological Virtues in the Minchiate's expanded sequence.

sections.Iconography: A woman nursing children, the image of Caritas.
sections.Meaning: Charity — the theological virtue of self-giving love, named the greatest of the three.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Carità (Charity) at trump XIX of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the third and highest Theological Virtue — depicted as a woman giving, nurturing children at her breast, or holding her heart toward others. The greatest of the three theological virtues according to Paul's Corinthians, Charity is positioned at XIX just before the elemental trumps that begin at XX.
sections.Symbol: Charity at trump XIX — the greatest theological virtue, giving and nurturing as the active expression of divine love at the apex of the Minchiate's virtue sequence.

sections.Iconography: A salamander in flames, or a burning scene — the element Fire.
sections.Meaning: Fire — will, transformation, the active spark. The first of the four classical elements unique to Minchiate among early decks.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: Il Fuoco (Fire) at trump XX of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the first of the four Elemental trumps — a unique feature of the Minchiate not found in standard tarot. Fire is depicted as a flame, a salamander (the mythical fire-creature), or a blazing brazier, the element in its conventional allegorical or symbolic form. The four elements (Fire, Water, Earth, Air at XX-XXIII) are a cosmological layer added to the Minchiate's already extended trump sequence.
sections.Symbol: Fire at trump XX — the first of the Minchiate's four elemental trumps, the fiery element rendered in allegorical form as a unique cosmological addition to the tarot tradition.

sections.Iconography: A figure by water, ships or fish — the element Water.
sections.Meaning: Water — feeling, flux, the depths; the passive, receptive element.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: L'Acqua (Water) at trump XXI of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the second elemental trump — depicted as waves, a water-bearer, or a figure associated with the flowing, receptive, and deep qualities of the water element. L'Acqua at XXI continues the four-element cosmological sequence unique to the Minchiate.
sections.Symbol: Water at trump XXI — the second elemental trump, the receptive and flowing element rendered in allegorical form in the Minchiate's cosmological sequence.

sections.Iconography: A landscape, a globe, or a figure on the land — the element Earth.
sections.Meaning: Earth — body, matter, the solid ground of the material world.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Terra (Earth) at trump XXII of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the third elemental trump — depicted as a figure of earthly stability, a globe, or an agricultural or geological symbol. La Terra at XXII represents the stable, material, and productive element in the Minchiate's cosmological trump sequence.
sections.Symbol: Earth at trump XXII — the third elemental trump, the stable and material element in the Minchiate's unique cosmological sequence.

sections.Iconography: Birds, clouds, or a figure with the winds — the element Air.
sections.Meaning: Air — mind, breath, the medium of exchange and thought.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: L'Aria (Air) at trump XXIII of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the fourth and final elemental trump — depicted as a figure of the atmosphere, a windblown form, or an angel or bird associated with the aerial element. L'Aria at XXIII completes the four-element cosmological sequence that distinguishes the Minchiate from all standard tarot traditions.
sections.Symbol: Air at trump XXIII — the fourth elemental trump, the atmosphere and breath rendered as allegorical figure, completing the cosmological sequence unique to the Minchiate.

sections.Iconography: The scales of Libra.
sections.Meaning: Libra — cardinal Air, ruled by Venus: balance, justice, partnership.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Bilancia (Libra / The Scales) at trump XXIV of the Minchiate Fiorentina is the first of 12 zodiac trumps — a unique feature of the Minchiate that adds the entire zodiacal sequence to its extended trump hierarchy. Libra is depicted as a female figure with scales, the astrological sign rendered as an allegorical figure or as the zodiacal symbol. The 12 zodiac trumps (XXIV-XXXV) form the most astronomically comprehensive layer of the Minchiate's 40-trump sequence.
sections.Symbol: Libra at trump XXIV — the first zodiac trump, the scales of balance as the opening of the Minchiate's unique 12-sign astronomical sequence.

sections.Iconography: The maiden of Virgo.
sections.Meaning: Virgo — mutable Earth, ruled by Mercury: discernment, service, the careful eye.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game (here the Florentine Minchiate) — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Vergine (Virgo / The Virgin) at trump XXV of the Minchiate Fiorentina depicts the zodiacal sign of Virgo — a female figure representing purity, discernment, and the harvest of the earth. The Vergine is shown in the allegorical style of 19th-century Florentine engraving, the astrological sign rendered as a personified figure.
sections.Symbol: Virgo at trump XXV — the Virgin zodiac sign, purity and discriminating harvest rendered as the second zodiacal figure in the Minchiate sequence.

sections.Iconography: The scorpion of Scorpio.
sections.Meaning: Scorpio — fixed Water, ruled by Mars: depth, intensity, transformation.

sections.Scene: La Stella (The Star) at trump XXXVI of the Minchiate Fiorentina shows the conventional Star card image — a nude or draped female figure pouring water beneath a large central star surrounded by smaller stars. After the 12 zodiac trumps, the celestial sequence of Star, Moon, Sun, and World begins. The Stella at XXXVI follows the Marseille Star's iconographic type in the 19th-century Florentine engraving style.

sections.Scene: Le Trombe (The Trumpets / Last Judgment) at trump XL — the highest-ranking trump of the Minchiate Fiorentina — shows the angel or angels with trumpets summoning the risen dead. In the Minchiate, the Last Judgment is the apex trump (XL), the highest and most powerful card in the deck's hierarchy, ranking above even the World (XXXIX). The Trombe are rendered as sounding trumpets of divine judgment calling all things to account at the end of time.
sections.Symbol: The Trumpets of Judgment at trump XL — the apex of the Minchiate hierarchy, the highest trump above even the World, the divine call to final account.

sections.About these Cards: Where ordinary tarot has the Popess, Empress, Emperor, Pope, and Love, the Minchiate has five ranked but near-identical 'Papi' (Popes). The Popess — the heretical card of the Visconti decks — is gone entirely. Church and Empire are flattened into one interchangeable office.
sections.Why this Matters: The disappearance of the Popess and the merging of the powers shows the tarot's imagery being sanitised and standardised as the deck spread through Counter-Reformation Italy.
sections.Identities: **EDITORIAL CALL (2026-06-13, maintainer-delegated):** The five lowest trumps are collectively the *Papi* (in-game *Papa One–Five*); their figures are I = Bagatto/Trivial Performer, II = Grand Duke, III = Western Emperor, IV = Eastern Emperor, V = Love. There is **no Pope** card and **no Popess/Empress** — Minchiate dropped them (Counter-Reformation Florence). [@web_minchiate_wp; @dummett2004]
sections.Scene: The five Papi (Popes) are the most distinctive innovation of the Minchiate Fiorentina — occupying trump positions I through V, replacing the standard tarot's Papess, Pope, Emperor, Empress, and Lovers with a sequence of five papal or imperial authority figures. The Papi are the weakest trumps in the Minchiate hierarchy, the base of the 40-trump sequence, a quintet of figures that reflects the Florentine political context of the 16th century in which the Minchiate emerged.
sections.Symbol: The five Papi — the Minchiate's unique replacement of standard early trumps with a quintet of papal figures, the weakest of the 40 trumps forming the base of the hierarchy.

sections.About these Cards: The Minchiate is the deck of the COMPLETE seven virtues. To the three cardinal virtues of ordinary tarot (Temperance, Fortitude, Justice) it restores the fourth — Prudence — and adds the three theological virtues, Faith, Hope, and Charity.
sections.Why this Matters: Only the Cary-Yale (with its three theological virtues) and the Minchiate (with all seven) treat the tarot as a complete moral cosmos. The Minchiate makes the virtue programme total.
sections.Scene: The Virtues of the Minchiate Fiorentina form the most comprehensive virtue sequence in any tarot tradition — four Cardinal Virtues (Temperance at VI, Fortitude at VII, Justice at VIII, Prudence at XVII) and three Theological Virtues (Hope at XVI, Faith at XVIII, Charity at XIX), totaling seven virtue trumps. No other surviving tarot includes all four Cardinal Virtues alongside the three Theological Virtues. The Minchiate's virtue sequence reflects the Florentine humanist and Christian tradition that produced this deck.
sections.Symbol: The seven Virtues of the Minchiate — four Cardinal plus three Theological, the most comprehensive virtue sequence in the tarot tradition, unique to the Minchiate's expanded trump sequence.

sections.About these Cards: Fire, Water, Earth, and Air enter the trump sequence as cards in their own right — a feature found in no standard tarot. With the twelve zodiac signs above them, the Minchiate builds a whole cosmology into the deck.
sections.How to Use: Read the elements as the building blocks beneath the zodiac: the four qualities from which the twelve signs are composed.
sections.Scene: The four Elements (Fire at XX, Water at XXI, Earth at XXII, Air at XXIII) form a unique cosmological group in the Minchiate Fiorentina — a layer of elemental trumps not found in any standard tarot tradition. The elements are placed after the Virtues and before the Zodiac signs, creating a cosmological sequence that expands the Minchiate's trump hierarchy to encompass the entire classical worldview: moral virtues, cosmic elements, astronomical signs, and celestial completion.
sections.Symbol: The four Elements at trumps XX-XXIII — a unique cosmological layer of the Minchiate, the classical elements Fire, Water, Earth, and Air as separate trump cards.

items.name: The Twelve Signs of the Zodiac

sections.About these Cards: The twelve zodiac signs form the longest stretch of the Minchiate trumps — a full astrological wheel set into the deck (in Minchiate's own idiosyncratic order, beginning with Libra). No other historical tarot contains them.
sections.How to Use: Read each as its sign — element, mode, and ruling planet — turning the deck into a portable zodiac.
sections.Scene: The twelve Zodiac signs (Libra at XXIV through Gemini at XXXV) form the largest single group in the Minchiate Fiorentina's unique trump sequence — twelve consecutive trumps representing the astrological signs of the zodiac, from Libra to Gemini. No other tarot tradition includes the complete zodiacal sequence as a group of trump cards. The zodiac trumps occupy the center of the Minchiate's 40-trump hierarchy, placed between the elemental layer and the final celestial sequence.
sections.Symbol: The twelve Zodiac signs at trumps XXIV-XXXV — the complete astrological cycle rendered as a unique group of consecutive trumps, the most astronomical element of the Minchiate's expanded sequence.

items.name: The Arie — The Five Supreme Cards

sections.About these Cards: The five highest cards — Star, Moon, Sun, World, and the Trumpets (Le Trombe, the Last Judgement) — are the 'Arie', unnumbered and supreme in play, crowning the whole 97-card edifice.
sections.How to Use: These are the deck's heavens: the celestial lights and the final trumpet above the zodiac, the elements, and all the world below.
sections.Scene: The final celestial sequence of the Minchiate Fiorentina — the Star (XXXVI), Moon (XXXVII), Sun (XXXVIII), World (XXXIX), and Last Judgment / Trumpets (XL) — forms the apex group of the 40-trump hierarchy. These five cards parallel the standard tarot's final celestial group but with the Last Judgment placed highest (at XL) rather than the World, a distinctive Minchiate innovation that places the divine summons above the cosmic completion.
sections.Symbol: The celestial apex group — Star, Moon, Sun, World, and Trumpets (XL), the five highest trumps of the Minchiate with the Last Judgment at the absolute apex.

sections.About this Suit: The Batons (Bastoni) of the Minchiate, suit of Fire. Each suit keeps the full court of four — and in Cups and Coins the page is a woman, while the knights ride as centaurs.
sections.How to Use: Read the suit for will in the everyday world beneath the great cosmological trumps.
sections.Scene: The batons suit of the Minchiate Fiorentina comprises Ace through 10 pip cards and four court ranks (Jack/Fante, Knight/Cavaliere, Queen/Regina, King/Re), totaling 14 cards. The batons suit of the Minchiate follows the Italian playing card convention of knotted clubs or staffs in geometric pip arrangements.
sections.Symbol: The Minchiate batons suit — 14 cards of creative fire in the Florentine Italian playing card tradition.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “minchiate” (41).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping identifying all items belonging to the Minchiate Fiorentina — the expanded 97-card Florentine game deck that added 19 extra trumps to the standard tarot's 22, creating a 41-trump sequence. The Minchiate originated in Florence in the 16th century (attributed variously to Leonardo da Vinci or the 1530s Florentine gaming culture) and remained popular in Florence and Tuscany until the 19th century. This edition dates to 1860-1890.
sections.Symbol: The Minchiate — the expanded Florentine game deck with 41 trumps, the most cosmologically comprehensive tarot tradition, dating from 16th-century Florence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “trionfi” (41).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for the trionfi (triumphs) — the 41 trump cards of the Minchiate Fiorentina, from Il Matto (0) through Le Trombe (40). The term 'trionfi' refers to the Roman triumph tradition that gave tarot its name, the allegorical procession of triumphal figures that constitutes the tarot's major arcana.
sections.Symbol: Trionfi — the 41 trump cards of the Minchiate, the complete set of triumphal allegorical figures from the Fool through the Trumpets.

sections.Symbol: The minor arcana — 56 suit cards in the Italian playing card tradition, the base of the Minchiate game hierarchy below the 41 trumps.

sections.Scene: The keyword axis of the Minchiate Fiorentina organizes the deck's 147 items by thematic grouping — the unique categories of the Minchiate (Papi, Virtues, Elements, Zodiac, celestial sequence), the four suits and their pip numbers, court ranks in Italian and English, and elemental associations.
